text: Iron complexes are amongst the most sought after substitutes for precious metals in photoactive hybrid systems. However, the fast decay rates of the charge transfer states often render iron-based luminescent and photoactive active species highly elusive. Emissive iron systems are rare and dual emission has so far not been reported in iron systems. In this contribution, the [Fe(ImP)2][PF6] compound, which is stabilised by using N-heterocyclic carbenes, where each iron ion is coordinated by four such N-hybridised carbon atoms and two cyclometalating aryl moieties (HImP stands for 1,1′-(1,3-phenylene)bis(3-methyl-1-imidazol-2-ylidene)), shows dual emission arising from Ligand to Metal Charge Transfer (LMCT) and Metal to Ligand Charge Transfer (MLCT) states, which illustrates a Janus-type behaviour. Low-lying π* states of the cyclometalating aryl rings result in the ligand part increase the energy of the MLCT state compared to that of other iron systems and, as a result, the MLCT-dominated state does not intersect with the ligand-based states, which are located much higher in energy. Although the MLCT-state has a relatively long lifetime of 4.6 ns it is strongly reducing as well as oxidizing and is therefore also capable of triggering electron transfer reactions for applications in photoredox catalysis.
